coredata.dc:title: MOFs-derived Co3O4@MnO2@Carbon dots with enhanced nanozymes activity for photoelectrochemical detection of cancer cells in whole blood
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Li-e
Xue, Linsheng
Li, Yuling
Ji, Jiangying
Yuan, Xinxin
Han, Hangchen
Ding, Lihua
Wu, Yongjun
Yang, Ruiying
coredata.dc:description: Nanozymes have attracted widespread attention, and rationally designing high-activity nanozymes to improve their application performance are a long-term objective. Herein, taking metal-organic frameworks-derived Co3O4 polyhedron with large surface area and high porosity as nanoconfinement carriers, Co3O4@MnO2@CDs polyhedron was successfully synthesized by the room-temperature reduction of MnO4 − ions and physical load of carbon dots (CDs). Through cancer cells-triggered double antibody sandwich strategy, the Co3O4@MnO2@CDs polyhedron were introduced to the TiO2 nanoparticle (NPs) modified electrode, leading to the decreased photocurrent. The Co3O4@MnO2@CDs polyhedron can not only quench the photocurrent of TiO2 NPs, also act as nanozymes to catalyze precipitates. Moreover, the precipitates can not only reduce the photoelectrochemical (PEC) response, also increase the quenching capacity of the Co3O4@MnO2@CDs polyhedron. Additionally, the steric hindrance effect of the Co3O4@MnO2@CDs-Ab conjugates further weaken the photocurrent. Based on the multifunctional Co3O4@MnO2@CDs polyhedron, the proposed PEC biosensor for the detection of A549 cancer cells exhibits a wide linear range from 102 to 106 cells/mL and a low detection limit of 11 cells/mL. Furthermore, this strategy can differentiate between lung cancer patients and healthy individuals. The designed multifunctional Co3O4@MnO2@CDs nanozymes provide a new horizon for PEC detection of cancer cells, and may have great potential in early clinical diagnosis and biomedical research.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanozymes
Co3O4@MnO2@CDs polyhedron
Multifunctional
Photoelectrochemical
Cancer cells

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Growth of MnO2 on the surface of Co3O4 polyhedron
Construction and detection of the PEC biosensor
Detection of A549 cells in whole blood
Enhanced peroxidase mimetics activity of Co3O4@MnO2@CDs polyhedron
Selectivity, stability and reproducibility of biosensor
PEC detection of A549 cells in whole blood

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Scientific and Technological Project of Henan Province
National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22104134), State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics ( Hunan University , 2021012 ), Scientific and Technological Project of Henan Province ( 232102311092 ), and Scientific Project of Henan Department of Education ( 22B330003 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of (A) ZIF-67, (B) Co3O4, and (C) Co3O4@MnO2 polyhedron. (D–E) TEM image, (F) EDS spectrum and (G) corresponding EDS mapping of Co3O4@MnO2@CDs polyhedron. (H) XRD patterns of ZIF-67 (a), Co3O4 (b), Co3O4@MnO2 (c), Co3O4@MnO2@CDs polyhedron (d).

(A) The survey spectrum, (B) C 1s, (C) Co 2p, and (D) O 1s XPS spectra of Co3O4@MnO2 polyhedron. (E) Nitrogen adsorption−desorption isotherms of the Co3O4@MnO2@CDs polyhedron. Inset plot is the corresponding pore size distribution. (F) UV–vis absorption spectra of Co3O4 (a), Co3O4@MnO2 (b), and Co3O4@MnO2@CDs polyhedron (c).

(A) Schematic illustration of oxidation color reaction and (B) absorbances at 652 nm of TMB by H2O2 catalyzed by blank (a), Co3O4 (b), Co3O4@MnO2 (c), Co3O4@MnO2@CDs (d). Error bars stand for the standard deviation of three independent experiments. (C) Photocurrent responses of ITO/TiO2/nanozymes before and after catalysis 4-CN into 4-CD precipitation with H2O2: ITO/TiO2/Co3O4 (a), ITO/TiO2/Co3O4@MnO2 (b), ITO/TiO2/Co3O4@MnO2@CDs (c), ITO/TiO2/Co3O4 with 4-CD (d), ITO/TiO2/Co3O4@MnO2 with 4-CD (e), ITO/TiO2/Co3O4@MnO2@CDs with 4-CD (f). (D) Photocurrent responses of ITO/TiO2/Co3O4 (a), ITO/TiO2/Co3O4 with 4-CD (d). (E) Photocurrent responses of ITO/TiO2/Co3O4@MnO2 (b), ITO/TiO2/Co3O4@MnO2 with 4-CD (e). (F) Photocurrent responses of ITO/TiO2/Co3O4@MnO2@CDs (c), ITO/TiO2/Co3O4@MnO2@CDs with 4-CD (f).

(A) Photocurrent responses and (B) EIS characterization in the different modified electrode: (a) ITO/TiO2, (b) ITO/TiO2/CS/Ab, (c) ITO/TiO2/CS/Ab/BSA, (d) ITO/TiO2/CS/Ab/BSA/A549 cells, (e) ITO/TiO2/CS/Ab/BSA/A549 cells/Co3O4@MnO2@CDs-Ab, (f) ITO/TiO2/CS/Ab/BSA/A549 cells/Co3O4@MnO2@CDs-Ab catalyze the oxidation of 4-CN into 4-CDs insoluble precipitation with the presence of H2O2.

(A) Photocurrent responses of the PEC biosensing platform toward various concentrations of A549 cells, from a to i: 1 × 102, 5 × 102, 1 × 103, 5 × 103, 1 × 104, 5 × 104, 1 × 105, 5 × 105 and 1 × 106 cells/mL. (B) Dependence of I on the A549 cell concentrations. Error bars stand for the standard deviation of three independent experiments.

(A) Selectivity of the developed PEC biosensor for A549 cells (105 cells/mL), HeLa, CCRF-CEM and their mixture, 105 cells/mL each. (B) Stability of the PEC biosensor toward A549 cells after being stored for 0, 7 and 14 days at 4 °C, (C) Reproducibility of the PEC biosensor. (D) Results from whole blood analysis of healthy people (samples number 1–3) and lung cancer patients (samples number 4–6). Error bars stand for the standard deviation of three independent experiments.
Schematic illustration of the PEC biosensor for the detection of cancer cells based on Co3O4@MnO2@CDs polyhedron with enhanced nanozymes activity.
Recovery tests of A549 cells in whole blood.

head.ce:title: MOFs-derived Co3O4@MnO2@Carbon dots with enhanced nanozymes activity for photoelectrochemical detection of cancer cells in whole blood

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Public Health, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou, 450001, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Public Health, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou, 450001, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Public Health, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanozymes have attracted widespread attention, and rationally designing high-activity nanozymes to improve their application performance are a long-term objective. Herein, taking metal-organic frameworks-derived Co3O4 polyhedron with large surface area and high porosity as nanoconfinement carriers, Co3O4@MnO2@CDs polyhedron was successfully synthesized by the room-temperature reduction of MnO4 − ions and physical load of carbon dots (CDs). Through cancer cells-triggered double antibody sandwich strategy, the Co3O4@MnO2@CDs polyhedron were introduced to the TiO2 nanoparticle (NPs) modified electrode, leading to the decreased photocurrent. The Co3O4@MnO2@CDs polyhedron can not only quench the photocurrent of TiO2 NPs, also act as nanozymes to catalyze precipitates. Moreover, the precipitates can not only reduce the photoelectrochemical (PEC) response, also increase the quenching capacity of the Co3O4@MnO2@CDs polyhedron. Additionally, the steric hindrance effect of the Co3O4@MnO2@CDs-Ab conjugates further weaken the photocurrent. Based on the multifunctional Co3O4@MnO2@CDs polyhedron, the proposed PEC biosensor for the detection of A549 cancer cells exhibits a wide linear range from 102 to 106 cells/mL and a low detection limit of 11 cells/mL. Furthermore, this strategy can differentiate between lung cancer patients and healthy individuals. The designed multifunctional Co3O4@MnO2@CDs nanozymes provide a new horizon for PEC detection of cancer cells, and may have great potential in early clinical diagnosis and biomedical research.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Lung cancer is one of the worst cancers worldwide, and the 5-year survival rate of lung cancer patients is only about 18%, while early detection can be significantly improved to about 80% [1]. Lung cancer has attracted widespread attention in the health field because of its early release of tumor cells from the primary site, infiltration of blood vessels, and metastasis to other body tissues [2]. Tumor cells in the blood are closely related to the degree of cancer progression, and 90% of cancer-related deaths are caused by cancer cell metastasis [3]. Therefore, early detection of cancer cells is essential for early screening, diagnosis and effective treatment of cancer [4]. Currently, available techniques in liquid biopsy for the detection of cancer cells, including electrochemistry, chemiluminescence, electrochemiluminescence, photoelectrochemistry [5–10].

Among these, photoelectrochemical (PEC) biosensor is an emerging and developing biomarker detection and analysis technology, which has attracted widespread attention due to its concentrated advantages of photochemical and electrochemical technologies, such as low background signal, simple operation, and easy miniaturization [11–17]. To improve its detection sensitivity, various signal amplification strategies have been utilized to construct high-performance PEC biosensors to detect cancer cells, including aptamer-based nucleic acid amplification techniques, synergistic bio-identification/spatial confinement, and changing photoelectric conversion efficiency [18–20]. As a new multifunctional signal amplification strategy, nanozymes combine the super characteristics of natural enzymes and nanomaterials, and have become a research hotspot in the field of biosensing, because of the advantages of low cost, strong catalytic activity, easy preservation and convenient preparation [21–25]. At the same time, the nanozyme-mediated PEC sensors have been validated with high efficiency and stability [26–30]. For example, Zheng et al. designed a PtPd/MnCo–CeO2 nanozyme-mediated synergistic amplification for PEC detection of cytokeratin 19 fragment 21–1 [30]. However, the nanozymes-based PEC biosensors for cancer cells assay have not been reported.

Carbon dots (CDs), a class of carbon-based nanomaterials widely used in biosensing, exhibit excellent biocompatibility, chemical stability, water solubility, easy-to-use preparation strategies, photoelectron transfer, and peroxidase-like activity [31–33]. Manganese dioxide nanoparticles (MnO2 NPs), as a p-type semiconductor, are an effective catalyst with high peroxidase activity, and has been widely used in the field of bioanalysis [34–36]. However, the easy aggregation of CDs or MnO2 NPs may affect the catalytic and PEC performance. Thus, the development of potential nanozymes is being actively sought. Recently, metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) and MOFs-derived hybrid materials have been proven to be a new type of multifunctional nanocarrier for bioanalysis, environmental monitoring, and energy, due to the large specific surface area, high pore structure, and easy functionalization [37–40]. Additionally, such material not only can prevent the aggregation of nanozymes effectively but also improve its catalytic performance through the synergy between different nanoparticles [41–43]. Therefore, combining the advantages of CDs, MnO2 NPs and MOFs, it is attractive to design a novel nanozyme for the PEC detection of cancer cells.

Herein, taking zeolite imidazole framework (ZIF-67) as precursor, Co3O4@MnO2 polyhedron with a large specific surface area and high porosity were synthesized by a simple room temperature reaction between KMnO4 and ZIF-67-derived Co3O4 polyhedron. Moreover, the Co3O4@MnO2@CDs polyhedron with enhanced nanozymes activity was achieved by loading CDs onto the Co3O4@MnO2 polyhedron. Due to the important role of A549 cells in the occurrence and development of lung cancer, a new PEC biosensor based on MOFs-derived Co3O4@MnO2@CDs polyhedron with enhanced nanozymes activity has been constructed for the detection of A549 cell (Scheme 1 ). The n-type semiconductor TiO2 NPs were used as the photoactive material and show excellent photocurrent response, and the Co3O4@MnO2@CDs polyhedron were modified on the PEC biosensing platform through the double antibody sandwich strategy. The Co3O4@MnO2@CDs polyhedron was used as the multifunction signal booster to reduce the PEC responses: (1) Co3O4@MnO2@CDs can act like peroxidase, effectively catalyzing 4-chloro-1-naphthol (4-CN) by H2O2 to produce benzene-4-chloro-hexadienone (4-CD) precipitate on the surface of the biosensor, which could weaken the photocurrents significantly (simulated enzyme catalyzed reaction); (2) the photocurrents of n-type TiO2 NPs can be weakened by the p-type semiconducting Co3O4@MnO2 as a result of the interaction between electron donors and the energy absorbed from the PEC system (p−n-type semiconductor quenching effect); (3) the quenching ability of Co3O4@MnO2 polyhedron can be enhanced by these 4-CD precipitates acting as electron acceptors; (4) Co3O4@MnO2@CDs-Ab conjugates exhibit steric hindrance effects, which impedes the electron transfer between the donor and the acceptor, leading to a weakening of the photocurrent. Thus, the ZIF-67-derived Co3O4@MnO2@CDs polyhedron with enhanced nanozymes activity as a multifunctional signal amplifier to establish PEC biosensor for cancer cells assay, may play a synergistic role with strong peroxidase activity and quenching n-type semiconductor signals, and have potential applications in biosensing and disease diagnosis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Growth of MnO2 on the surface of Co3O4 polyhedron
ce:section.ce:para: A typical experiment procedure for the preparation of ZIF-67 polyhedron was provided in the Supporting Material [44]. The as-prepared ZIF-67 polyhedron was heated to 350 °C at a rate of 5 °C/min in a tube furnace, and kept for 30 min. Subsequently, the nitrogen was turned off and allowed to stand in air for 30 min to obtain Co3O4 polyhedron. For growth of MnO2 on the surface of Co3O4 polyhedron, a similar synthesized approach in previous report was performed [45]. KMnO4 (10 mg) was dissolved in KOH solution (30 mL, 0.1 M), and followed by the addition of Co3O4 (20 mg) with vigorous agitation. After 12 h, the obtained product (Co3O4@MnO2) was washed by centrifugation and dried below vacuum at 60 °C for 12 h.

The Co3O4@MnO2@CDs polyhedron were synthesized as follows: the Co3O4@MnO2 solution (2 mL, 2 mg/mL) and as-prepared CDs solution (8 mL, Supporting Material) were mixed, and then shaken for 12 h at room temperature. After centrifugation to removing the supernatant, the obtained product was dispersed in PBS solution (2 mL) to obtain a Co3O4@MnO2@CDs solution.

To prepare Co3O4@MnO2@CDs-Ab conjugates, EDC/NHS (400 μL) was added to the above Co3O4@MnO2@CDs solution for 30 min of activation at room temperature. Subsequently, the mixed solution was reacted with anti-Epithelial cell adhesion molecule (anti-EpCAM, Ab, 1 mL, 60 μg/mL) for 12 h and repeated the purification operation. Then, 2% wt BSA solution was used to block the remaining active sites. Finally, the purified Co3O4@MnO2@CDs-Ab conjugates were dissolved in PBS (2 mL) and stored at 4 °C for backup.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction and detection of the PEC biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The indium tin oxide (ITO) electrode was ultrasonically cleaned using 1 M NaOH aqueous solution and 1 M NaOH ethanol-water (1:1, vv) solution for 20 min, respectively. First, 25 μL of TiO2 NPs solution (1.5 mg/mL) was dropped onto the ITO electrode and then allowed to dry at room temperature to yield the ITO/TiO2 electrode. After 20 μL of chitosan (CS) solution was dried on the electrode surface naturally, 20 μL of glutaraldehyde (GLD) solution (2.5%) was added and reacted for 30 min to obtain ITO/TiO2/CS electrode. Subsequently, the obtained electrode was modified with 20 μL of Ab solution (20 μg/mL) at room temperature to obtain ITO/TiO2/CS/Ab electrode. Then, the residual active sites were blocked by the addition of 20 μL of BSA solution (ITO/TiO2/CS/Ab/BSA electrode). After different concentrations of cancer cells were added, the target cells were captured on the electrode by antibody antigen specific binding. After that, Co3O4@MnO2@CDs-Ab conjugates were immobilized on the modified electrode by the bispecific sandwiching strategy on the target cells. Next, the obtain ITO/TiO2/CS/Ab/BSA/A549/Co3O4@MnO2@CDs-Ab electrode was reacted with 20 μL mixed solution (10 mM 4-CN and 1 mM H2O2) to generate precipitate 4-CD. Finally, the PEC assay was performed in Tris-HCl buffer solution (pH 7.4, 0.1 M) containing 0.1 M ascorbic acid (AA) at 0 V.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of A549 cells in whole blood
ce:section.ce:para: The whole blood samples were the remaining samples from clinical testing of lung cancer patients and healthy persons. This work was conducted in agreement with the World Medical Association's code of ethics, and was approved by “Zhengzhou University Life Science Ethics Review Committee” (Code: ZZURIB G2R 2021–126).

The morphology and structure of the prepared samples were carefully examined by typical scanning electron microscope (SEM) and transmission electron microscope (TEM) images. As shown in Fig. 1 A and B, the ZIF-67 polyhedron has a smooth surface and a uniformly shaped dodecahedral structure, while the Co3O4 polyhedron after carbonization obtains a concave dodecahedral with rough surface. After growth of MnO2 on the surface of Co3O4 polyhedron, Co3O4@MnO2 polyhedron still maintains a concave dodecahedral structure, but its surface is significantly different from that of pure Co3O4 polyhedron, and the opacity is increased (Fig. 1C). The TEM images (Fig. 1D and E) show the presence of smaller size spherical particles on the surface of the Co3O4@MnO2 polyhedron, indicating that Co3O4@MnO2@CDs polyhedron is successfully synthesized. In addition, the elemental compositions of the Co3O4@MnO2@CDs polyhedron have been investigated by EDS analysis. As shown in Fig. 1F, it can be observed that the as-prepared polyhedron has five elements (Co, O, Mn, C and N), which indicates that CDs are successfully bound to the surface of Co3O4@MnO2 polyhedron, and the EDS mapping image shows that Co, O, Mn, and C elements are distributed uniformly on the dodecahedron (Fig. 1G).

To further confirm the successful synthesis of Co3O4@MnO2@CDs polyhedron, XRD analysis was carried out (Fig. 1H). As shown in Fig. 1H (curve a), all diffraction peaks of the as-prepared ZIF-67 are consistent with those previously reported [46]. After calcination, Co3O4 polyhedron (curve b) shows some new diffraction peaks at 18.97°, 31.27, 36.85°, 39.85°, 44.85°, 55.65°, 59.42°, 65.27° and 77.36°, which correspond to the (111), (220), (311), (222), (400), (422), (511), (440) and (533) facets of Co3O4 (JCPDS, PDF#76–1802), respectively. After the formation of MnO2, two new diffraction peaks appear at 31.27° and 50.23°, which can be indexed with the diffraction of MnO2 [47,48]. The XRD pattern of Co3O4@MnO2@CDs polyhedron (curve d) displays a broad peak centered at 24.3°, which provides evidence for the existence of CDs in the Co3O4@MnO2@CDs polyhedron [49]. Co3O4 exists at a peak of 36.85° and the peaks at other locations disappear, due to the broad peak of CDs [50]. Additionally, the weak peak located at 72.55° originates from carbon [45].

XPS analysis was further carried out to investigate the elemental compositions and chemical states of Co3O4@MnO2 polyhedron, and the results were shown in Fig. 2 A–D. From Fig. 2A, the survey spectrum confirms the presence of Co, O, Mn, C and N elements in the sample, while the N element may come from the residual carbon from pyrolysis of ZIF-67. As shown in Fig. 2B, three peaks centered at 284.8, 285.9, and 288.4 eV are assigned to the C–C, C–N/C–O, and OC–O groups, respectively. The C–C is ascribed to the standard peak of carbon for calibration, while the C–N/C–O and OC–O groups are stemmed from the residual carbon from pyrolysis of ZIF-67 [30]. The two peaks at 779.7 and 794.8 eV correspond to the Co 2p3/2 and Co 2p1/2 spin orbitals, respectively (Fig. 2C). Moreover, the fitted peaks at 779.7 and 794.7 eV are attributed to Co3+, whereas the other two peaks (at 781.0 and 796.2 eV) are attributed to Co2+, which suggests the formation of Co3O4 [51]. From Fig. 2D, the peak at 530.1 eV in the high-resolution O 1s spectrum corresponds to the O2− ions comprising the Co3O4@MnO2 polyhedron, while the peaks at 531.5 and 532.8 eV belong to the oxygen vacancy and water molecules absorbed on the polyhedron surface, respectively [52].

To delve the porous structure and specific surface area, the Co3O4@MnO2 polyhedron was explored via N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms. As shown in Fig. 2E and the corresponding inset, the Brunauer−Emmett−Teller specific surface area and the average pore size of the Co3O4@MnO2 polyhedron is 68.1 m2/g and 10.6 nm, respectively. Due to its polyhedral morphology, porous structure, and high specific surface area, Co3O4@MnO2 polyhedron can be used as an effective carrier to load CDs and provide enough catalytic active sites [53]. Additionally, Fig. 2F presents the UV–vis absorption spectra of Co3O4@MnO2@CDs polyhedron. The growth of MnO2 on the surface of Co3O4 polyhedron (curve a) leads to a significant red-shift of the absorption peak compared to Co3O4 polyhedron (curve b). Moreover, a new maximum absorption band in the curve c appears at 352 nm corresponding to a typical absorption peak of CDs, which further indicates that CDs are successfully modified on the Co3O4@MnO2 polyhedron [49].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhanced peroxidase mimetics activity of Co3O4@MnO2@CDs polyhedron
ce:section.ce:para: The enhanced peroxidase mimetics activity of Co3O4@MnO2@CDs polyhedron was investigated in the catalysis of 3,3′,5,5′-Tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) as substrate [54,55]. As described in Fig. 3 A, Co3O4, Co3O4@MnO2, and Co3O4@MnO2@CDs polyhedron can catalyze the oxidation of TMB in the presence of H2O2 to generate a typical blue color with the typical absorbance peak of oxidation products of TMB at 652 nm [56,57]. Also, Co3O4@MnO2@CDs polyhedron shows the much stronger activity of peroxidase mimetics and higher absorbance at 652 nm than that of Co3O4 or Co3O4@MnO2 polyhedron (Fig. 3A and B). Additionally, the influence of different nanozymes catalyzed precipitation on the photocurrent of modified electrode was further investigated and the corresponding results were shown in Fig. 3C–F. It is noted that the photocurrents decrease after the introduction of different nanozymes, indicating that the as-prepared nanozymes can catalyze 4-CN to produce 4-CD insoluble precipitation with the help of H2O2 on the modified electrode. Obviously, the catalytic precipitation from Co3O4@MnO2@CDs polyhedron produces the largest photocurrent attenuation, which means that it has the enhanced peroxidase mimetics catalytic precipitation ability and is beneficial to the construction of PEC biosensors for cancer cells assay.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) is an effective method for verifying the preparation process of PEC biosensor. Fig. 4 A shows the EIS profiles of the interfacial charge transfer resistance (R ct) of the electrodes at different stages of assembly. The ITO/TiO2 electrode displays minimal electron transfer resistance (curve a). Nevertheless, the gradual introduction of Ab and BSA onto the electrode surface lead to escalating R ct values (curves b and c). When the cancer cells are incubated with the biosensor, R ct value increases significantly (curve d), because of the evident steric hindrance of cells bound on the electrode. After the modification of Co3O4@MnO2@CDs polyhedron on the obtained electrode, the R ct value further increases, which can be explained by the weak charge transfer of Co3O4@MnO2@CDs semiconductors (curve e). Finally, the Co3O4@MnO2@CDs nanozyme-catalyzed 4-CN to produce 4-CD insoluble precipitation with the help of H2O2 leads to an increase in R ct value (curve f), due to the insoluble precipitate of 4-CD to obstruct the negatively charged [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− probe from reaching the electrode interface. The observed alterations in EIS spectra confirm the effective building of the PEC biosensor.

The photocurrent response at different stages of the assembly process were tracked (Fig. 4B). The initial ITO/TiO2 electrode is measured with a large photocurrent (curve a). After Ab and BSA are assembled, the photocurrents decrease successively because of the poor conductivity (curves b and c). After the A549 cells are captured by the antibodies on the electrodes, the photocurrent decreases significantly, which may be affected by the poor conductivity of cells (curve d), demonstrating that the biosensor is successful in capturing the target cells. The electron transport process is affected by the photocurrent quenching effect of the p-type Co3O4@MnO2@CDs polyhedron on n-type TiO2 NPs and the steric hindrance effect of poor-conductivity Co3O4@MnO2@CDs polyhedron, resulting in further weakening of the photocurrent (curve e). The reduction of photocurrent demonstrates that Co3O4@MnO2@CDs polyhedron can be acted as nanozymes with peroxidase activity, which effectively catalyze 4-CN to produce 4-CD insoluble precipitation with the help of H2O2, thereby preventing AA from supplying electrons (curve f). These results demonstrate the feasibility of Co3O4@MnO2@CDs nanozyme-based PEC biosensors for cancer cells assay.

Under optimal experimental conditions (Fig. S1), the PEC biosensor based on Co3O4@MnO2@CDs with enhanced nanozymes activity was proposed for quantitative A549 cells detection. From Fig. 5 A, the photocurrent decreases correspondingly with the increase of A549 cells concentration ranging from 1 × 102 to 1 × 106 cells/mL. As shown in Fig. 5B, there is a well-behaved linear relationship between the photocurrent and the logarithm of the A549 cells concentration ranging from 1 × 102 to 1 × 106 cells/mL, and the linear fitting equation is the photocurrent (μA) = 29.4375–3.6486 lgC (cells/mL) (R 2 = 0.9961), with the limit detection of 11 cells/mL (S/N = 3). Moreover, to the best of our knowledge, the LOD of the developed PEC strategy for cancer cells detection based on MOFs-derived multifunctional Co3O4@MnO2@CDs nanozymes is much lower than that of most other PEC strategies (Table S1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity, stability and reproducibility of biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: To further explore the selectivity of the designed PEC biosensor, EpCAM-negatively expressed human cervical cancer cells (HeLa) and human acute T lymphoblastic leukemia cells (CCRF-CEM) were selected as possible interfering cells. From Fig. 6 A, the photocurrent is no significant change in the presence of only HeLa cells or CCFR-CEM cells compared with the blank. However, when the A549 cells (105 cells/mL) exist, the photocurrent decreases significantly, suggesting that the A549 cells can be specifically captured through antigen-antibody binding. When the target A549 cells are separately mixed with HeLa cells and CCFR-CEM cells, the corresponding photocurrents decrease obviously, and are consistent with that of the pure target cells. These results indicate that the developed nanozyme biosensor has satisfactory selectivity for the detection of A549 cells.

Furthermore, the stability of the developed biosensor was investigated to ensure its practical applications. As displayed in Fig. 6B, after 7 and 14 days of storage 4 °C, the biosensor toward A549 cells (105 cells/mL) can maintain 99.9% and 99.1% of the initial photocurrent response. In addition, five modified electrodes fabricated under the same procedure are used for the detection of cancer cells (103 cells/mL), and the relative standard deviation (RSD) is calculated to be 2.7% (Fig. 6C). Based on these results, the fabricated nanozyme PEC biosensor has good storage stability and reproducibility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PEC detection of A549 cells in whole blood
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the applicability and reliability of the developed PEC biosensor, the recovery tests for different A549 cells concentrations were performed in the whole blood sample from healthy people. As displayed in Table 1 , A549 cells with different concentrations (103, 104, and 105 cells/mL) are added in the 10-fold diluted the whole blood sample solutions, and the average recoveries for the added A549 cells are 94.1%, 100.2%, and 102.4%, respectively. These data indicate that the proposed PEC biosensor can be effectively applied to detect A549 cells in complex biological samples.

To further verify the developed nanozyme strategy used for quantitative detection cancer cells in practical settings, the whole blood samples from both lung cancer patients and healthy individuals were analyzed. As shown in Fig. 6D, the photocurrent response is significantly lower in lung cancer samples compared to the normal samples (P < 0.05). The results demonstrate that the proposed nanozyme PEC biosensor can distinguish the lung cancer patients from healthy people.

In summary, based on MOFs-derived Co3O4@MnO2@CDs nanozymes as the multifunctional signal amplifier, a highly sensitive and selective PEC biosensor is developed for the detection of cancer cells. The Co3O4@MnO2@CDs polyhedron serves as a powerful quencher, reducing the photocurrent of n-type TiO2 by outcompeting the consumption of electron donors and light energy. Furthermore, Co3O4@MnO2@CDs polyhedron acts as peroxidase mimetics, efficiently producing catalytic precipitates. Importantly, these precipitates not only modify the surface of the biosensor to inhibit the sensing reaction, but play the role of electron acceptors to enhance the quenching ability of Co3O4@MnO2@CDs. Moreover, the steric hindrance effect of Co3O4@MnO2@CDs-Ab conjugates further weaken the PEC response. The proposed PEC biosensor for the detection of cancer cells shows a wide linear response range from 1 × 102 to 1 × 106 cells/mL and a low LOD of 11 cell/mL, with good selectivity, stability and reproducibility. More importantly, the proposed strategy has satisfactory recoveries and can distinguish lung cancer patients from healthy individuals (P < 0.05), indicating that this nanozyme PEC biosensor has great potential applications in cancer cell practical analysis. The developed multifunctional Co3O4@MnO2@CDs nanozymes offer a novel strategy for detecting cancer cells, and further expand the application of nanozyme in PEC cancer cells analysis.

Li-e Liu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Linsheng Xue: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Yuling Li: Methodology, Investigation. Jiangying Ji: Investigation, Formal analysis. Xinxin Yuan: Validation, Conceptualization. Hangchen Han: Conceptualization. Lihua Ding: Validation. Yongjun Wu: Investigation. Ruiying Yang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22104134), State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics (Hunan University, 2021012), Scientific and Technological Project of Henan Province (232102311092), and Scientific Project of Henan Department of Education (22B330003).
